title: Vichyssoise
subtitle: Chilled leek and potato soup, smooth, cold, and finished with cream
description: Vichyssoise is a cold leek and potato soup: pale, silky-smooth, and finished with double cream. It is served chilled, making it the rare French dish where advance preparation is not just acceptable but required. The soup is simple to make — the technique is identical to a standard leek and potato soup — but the quality of the leeks and a good hand blender make the difference between a good soup and a great one.
sourceNotes: Vichyssoise is generally attributed to Louis Diat, a French chef working at the Ritz-Carlton Hotel in New York City, who created the dish around 1917 based on a leek and potato soup his mother made in Vichy, France. The recipe appears in Diat's Cooking à la Ritz (1941).

recipeTools.notes: For blending the soup smooth in the pan. A standing blender also works — blend in small batches with the lid slightly ajar for safety.

glossaryTerms.definition: A member of the allium family, milder and sweeter than onion. The pale white and light green parts are used in vichyssoise; the dark green tops are too fibrous for a refined soup but can be used in stock.

ingredients.notes: White and pale green parts only, washed and sliced into rounds
Maris Piper or King Edward — peeled and roughly diced
Or vegetable stock for a vegetarian version
Plus a little more to serve
White pepper rather than black — keeps the soup pale

steps: Melt the butter in a large saucepan over a medium-low heat. Add the leeks and onion with a pinch of salt. Cook gently for 10–12 minutes, stirring occasionally, until completely soft but not coloured.
Add the diced potato and pour in the stock. Bring to a simmer and cook for 20–25 minutes until the potato is tender when pierced.

Blend the soup until completely smooth — use a hand blender in the pan or transfer in batches to a standing blender. If using a standing blender, leave the lid slightly ajar and hold it with a folded tea towel to release steam pressure.
Pass the blended soup through a fine sieve into a large bowl, pressing gently with the back of a ladle. This step produces the silky texture characteristic of vichyssoise — do not skip it.
Stir in the cream and season well with salt and white pepper.
Cover and refrigerate for at least 3 hours, or overnight. The soup thickens as it cools — thin with a little cold stock or cream if needed before serving.
Serve in cold bowls with a swirl of cream and a scattering of snipped chives.

content.text: Vichyssoise must be chilled before serving — allow at least 3 hours in the fridge. It is an ideal make-ahead soup. The sieving step after blending is what gives it the characteristic pale, satiny consistency; a blended-but-unsieved leek and potato soup is still good but is a different dish.
Melt {{butter-unsalted}} of butter in a large saucepan over a medium-low heat. Add {{leek}} of sliced

and the onion with a pinch of salt. Cook gently for 10–12 minutes until completely soft but not coloured. Add {{floury-potato}} of diced potato and {{chicken-stock}} of stock. Simmer for 20–25 minutes until the potato is tender.
Blend until completely smooth. Pass through a fine sieve into a large bowl, pressing gently. Stir in {{double-cream}} of cream. Season with salt and white pepper. Refrigerate for at least 3 hours.
Before serving, check the consistency — the soup thickens as it chills. Thin with a splash of cold stock or cream if needed. Serve in chilled bowls with a swirl of cream and snipped chives.
